boundary_scope.in_scope: Objects move when pushed or pulled. Magnets attract some metals and can attract or repel each other.
boundary_scope.deferred: Force vectors, electromagnetic field theory, gravitational equations.

boundary_scope.in_scope: Plants need sunlight, water, and soil. Animals need food, water, and shelter. Both are living things that grow.
boundary_scope.deferred: Photosynthesis chemistry, food webs, cell biology.

boundary_scope.in_scope: Environments have living and non-living things. Environments change with weather and seasons; animals and plants can adapt or move.
boundary_scope.deferred: Climate change mechanisms, ecological succession, keystone species.

boundary_scope.in_scope: Weather describes outdoor conditions (sunny, cloudy, rainy, windy, snowy). Sunlight warms the Earth. Severe weather can be dangerous.
boundary_scope.deferred: Meteorology instruments, pressure systems, climate modelling.

assumed_experience: playing outside in sun and rain

boundary_scope.in_scope: The body has five senses: sight (eyes), hearing (ears), smell (nose), taste (tongue), touch (skin). Each sense gives information about the world.
boundary_scope.deferred: Neuroscience, sensory processing disorders, sense organ anatomy.

core_concept: Spatial Sense: Maps, Globes, and World Features

boundary_scope.in_scope: A map is a flat picture of a place; a globe is a round model of Earth. Earth has oceans, land, rivers, lakes, mountains, and poles.
boundary_scope.deferred: Map projections, coordinate systems, topography.

assumed_experience: seeing a world map or globe
looking at pictures of mountains and oceans

boundary_scope.in_scope: Earth has seven continents: Asia, Europe, Africa, North America, South America, Antarctica, and Australia. Each is a large landmass surrounded mostly by water.
boundary_scope.deferred: Plate tectonics, country geography, biome differences between continents.

core_concept: My Geography: Town, State, and Country

boundary_scope.in_scope: I live in a town in a state. The United States is made up of 50 states. A city is a large town.
boundary_scope.deferred: State capitals, political history, federalism, US territories.

boundary_scope.in_scope: Native Americans lived in North America before Europeans arrived. Different groups lived in different regions with different homes, foods, and traditions.
boundary_scope.deferred: Tribal governance, forced removal, contemporary Native communities.

assumed_experience: seeing pictures of Native American homes or clothing

boundary_scope.in_scope: In 1492, Christopher Columbus sailed from Europe on the Niña, Pinta, and Santa María and reached the Americas, which Europeans called the New World.
boundary_scope.deferred: Colonialism consequences, indigenous impact, contested historical narrative.

assumed_experience: knowing explorers travel to find new places

boundary_scope.in_scope: Pilgrims sailed from England on the Mayflower and landed at Plymouth. They shared a harvest feast with the Wampanoag people; this became Thanksgiving.
boundary_scope.deferred: Religious persecution background, colonial hardships, historical accuracy debates.

boundary_scope.in_scope: July 4 is Independence Day in the United States, celebrating freedom. Democracy means people choose their leaders. Slavery was a period when some people were not free.
boundary_scope.deferred: Constitutional mechanics, abolitionist movement, Civil War causes.

core_concept: Elements of Art: Color, Line, Sculpture, Architecture

boundary_scope.in_scope: Art uses color and line to create images. Sculpture is three-dimensional art. Architecture is the design of buildings.
boundary_scope.deferred: Art history movements, formal art criticism, restoration techniques.

boundary_scope.in_scope: Music has rhythm (beat), melody (tune), and dynamics (loud/soft). Songs can tell stories or express feelings.
boundary_scope.deferred: Music notation, formal composition theory, instrument families.

core_concept: Poetry and Fables: Mother Goose and Aesop

boundary_scope.in_scope: Poetry uses rhythm and rhyme. Nursery rhymes (Mother Goose) are short poems for children. Fables (Aesop) use animal characters to teach a lesson (moral).
boundary_scope.deferred: Poetic meter analysis, cross-cultural folklore scholarship.

core_concept: Sun, Moon, and Stars: Predictable Patterns

boundary_scope.in_scope: The Sun rises in the east and sets in the west. The Moon has phases that repeat in a pattern. Day and night are caused by Earth's rotation.
boundary_scope.deferred: Stellar fusion, lunar geology, orbital mechanics equations.

assumed_experience: watching the sun rise and set
seeing the moon change shape over weeks

core_concept: Plant and Animal Survival: Structure, Function, Growth

boundary_scope.in_scope: Body structures help living things survive. Animals respond to stimuli and grow from young to adult. Offspring resemble their parents.
boundary_scope.deferred: Genetics, evolutionary theory, molecular biology.

boundary_scope.in_scope: Light travels in straight lines and can be reflected or absorbed. Shadows form when objects block light. Sound is created by vibrations and travels through materials.
boundary_scope.deferred: Wave equations, electromagnetic spectrum, decibel scale.

boundary_scope.in_scope: Simple machines (lever, wheel/axle, pulley, inclined plane, wedge, screw) make work easier by changing the direction or size of a force.
boundary_scope.deferred: Mechanical advantage calculations, compound machines, friction coefficients.

core_concept: Human Body Systems: Skeletal, Muscular, Respiratory, Circulatory, Nervous

boundary_scope.in_scope: The skeletal and muscular systems support and move the body. Lungs take in air (respiratory); the heart pumps blood (circulatory). The brain and nerves control the body (nervous system).
boundary_scope.deferred: Biochemistry, disease mechanisms, surgical procedures, organ transplants.

assumed_experience: knowing they have bones and muscles

boundary_scope.in_scope: Geographic terms describe Earth's surface: peninsula, island, strait, bay, delta, plateau, valley, plain, coast, glacier. Maps use symbols and keys.
boundary_scope.deferred: Topographic mapping, GIS, geological formation processes.

assumed_experience: looking at maps with different colors and shapes

core_concept: Early World Civilizations: Mesopotamia, Egypt, and World Religions

boundary_scope.in_scope: The earliest civilizations formed in Mesopotamia (Tigris-Euphrates) and Egypt (Nile). They developed writing, farming, and cities. Major world religions began in ancient times.
boundary_scope.deferred: Archaeological methods, doctrinal details, geopolitical analysis.

boundary_scope.in_scope: Mexico is a country south of the United States with rich traditions, foods, and festivals. It has ancient ruins from indigenous civilizations.
boundary_scope.deferred: Political history, economic systems, immigration policy.

core_concept: Early Americans: Nomads and Indigenous Civilizations

boundary_scope.in_scope: The first Americans came from Asia long ago. Hunter-nomads followed animals for food. Some groups settled and built civilizations with farming, cities, and traditions.
boundary_scope.deferred: Genetic migration studies, tribal governance details, colonial impact.

assumed_experience: knowing people lived in America before Columbus

boundary_scope.in_scope: After Columbus, Spanish conquistadors explored and conquered parts of the Americas. English settlers came to North America seeking a new life and religious freedom.
boundary_scope.deferred: Colonization consequences, disease impact, detailed political history.

boundary_scope.in_scope: American colonists were ruled by England and fought the Revolutionary War to govern themselves. The US became independent; George Washington became the first president.
boundary_scope.deferred: Constitutional debates, economic causes, Loyalist perspectives, military strategy.

boundary_scope.in_scope: After independence, Americans moved west across the frontier. American symbols include the flag, Statue of Liberty, bald eagle, and Liberty Bell.
boundary_scope.deferred: Manifest destiny ideology, Native American displacement, political symbolism.

assumed_experience: hearing about famous Americans in stories

core_concept: Art from Long Ago: Elements, Portraits, Murals, Architecture

boundary_scope.in_scope: Art elements include color, line, shape, and texture. Portraits show faces; still life shows objects; murals are large wall paintings. Architecture designs buildings to be beautiful and functional.
boundary_scope.deferred: Formal art criticism, art conservation, architectural engineering.

core_concept: Music Elements, Musical Terms, and American Musical Traditions

boundary_scope.in_scope: Music has pitch, tempo, and dynamics. Music can tell a story. American musical traditions include folk songs, work songs, and spirituals.
boundary_scope.deferred: Music notation, formal composition, music history movements.

assumed_experience: listening to music with different speeds

core_concept: Aesop's Fables, Comparative Folklore, Literary Terms

boundary_scope.in_scope: Fables teach lessons through animal characters. Different cultures have similar story types (e.g., Cinderella across cultures). Literary terms: plot, character, setting.
boundary_scope.deferred: Cross-cultural literary analysis, oral tradition scholarship.

core_concept: Properties of Matter: States, Heating, Cooling, Building

boundary_scope.in_scope: Matter has properties (color, texture, hardness) and three states (solid, liquid, gas). Heating can melt or evaporate matter; cooling can freeze or condense it. Materials can be combined to build structures.
boundary_scope.deferred: Atomic theory, chemical formulas, thermodynamics equations.

core_concept: Organisms and Their Habitats: Plants, Animals, Ecosystems

boundary_scope.in_scope: A habitat is where an organism lives and gets what it needs. Plants and animals are diverse and suited to their habitats; they depend on each other through pollination and food. An ecosystem is a community of living and non-living things.
boundary_scope.deferred: Food web mathematics, keystone species, biome classification.

assumed_experience: seeing plants and animals in different places
knowing animals eat plants or other animals

core_concept: Exploring Land and Water: Landforms, Earth's Water, Erosion

boundary_scope.in_scope: Earth's surface has landforms (mountains, valleys, plains, canyons, deltas). Most of Earth is water (oceans, lakes, rivers, glaciers). Wind and water cause erosion, slowly changing the land.
boundary_scope.deferred: Plate tectonics, rock cycle, hydrological cycle equations.

boundary_scope.in_scope: Electricity flows through circuits (power source, wires, device, switch). Magnets have two poles; like poles repel, opposite poles attract. Electricity must be used safely.
boundary_scope.deferred: Ohm's law, alternating vs. direct current, electromagnetic theory.

core_concept: Human Body: Cells, Tissues, Digestion, Excretion

boundary_scope.in_scope: The body is made of cells that form tissues and organs. The digestive system breaks food into nutrients. The excretory system removes waste.
boundary_scope.deferred: Cell biology, biochemistry, microbiome, genetic regulation.

assumed_experience: knowing food goes to the stomach

core_concept: Spatial Sense: Coordinates, Hemispheres, and Climate Regions

boundary_scope.in_scope: Latitude and longitude form a grid to locate places. The equator divides Earth into Northern and Southern Hemispheres. Climate regions include tropics, temperate zones, and polar regions.
boundary_scope.deferred: Coordinate calculations, GIS, time zones, navigation.

core_concept: Early Asian Civilizations: India and China

boundary_scope.in_scope: Ancient India developed along the Indus and Ganges rivers; Hinduism and Buddhism began here. Ancient China developed along the Yellow River and created silk, paper, and the Great Wall.
boundary_scope.deferred: Sanskrit language, dynastic politics, Silk Road economics.

assumed_experience: seeing pictures of the Great Wall

core_concept: Modern Japanese Civilization: Geography and Culture

boundary_scope.in_scope: Japan is an island nation in East Asia with mountains and volcanoes. Japanese culture includes traditional arts (origami, kabuki), festivals, and distinctive cuisine.
boundary_scope.deferred: Meiji Restoration, WWII role, government structure.

boundary_scope.in_scope: Ancient Greece had city-states (Athens, Sparta). Greeks developed democracy, philosophy, and the Olympics. Greek myths told stories of gods and heroes that explained the world.
boundary_scope.deferred: Detailed political philosophy, Greek language, archaeological analysis.

boundary_scope.in_scope: The Constitution is the supreme law of the US. It creates three branches: legislative (makes laws), executive (carries out laws), judicial (interprets laws). The Bill of Rights protects freedoms.
boundary_scope.deferred: Constitutional amendments, judicial review mechanics, separation of powers theory.

assumed_experience: knowing the president is a leader

boundary_scope.in_scope: The War of 1812 was fought between the US and Britain over independence and trade rights. The national anthem was written during this war.
boundary_scope.deferred: Detailed military campaigns, causes beyond trade rights, Canadian perspective.

assumed_experience: knowing the US fought Britain before

core_concept: Civil War, Reconstruction, and Westward Expansion

boundary_scope.in_scope: The Civil War was fought between the Union (North) and Confederacy (South) over slavery. Abraham Lincoln led the Union; slavery was abolished after the war. Americans then moved west as pioneers.
boundary_scope.deferred: Reconstruction politics, detailed military campaigns, economic causes of secession.

core_concept: Immigration, Citizenship, and Fighting for a Cause

boundary_scope.in_scope: Immigrants move from one country to another seeking freedom or opportunity. Citizens have rights and responsibilities. Reformers fought to change unjust conditions.
boundary_scope.deferred: Immigration law, civil rights legislation, national origin statistics.

boundary_scope.in_scope: North America includes the US, Canada, and Mexico. South America lies to the south. Major features: Rocky Mountains, Andes Mountains, Amazon River, Caribbean Sea.
boundary_scope.deferred: Detailed country geography, climate zones, economic geography.

assumed_experience: seeing a map of the Americas

core_concept: Art Styles: Elements, Landscape, Abstraction, Sculpture, Architecture

boundary_scope.in_scope: Landscape art depicts outdoor scenes. Abstraction uses shapes and colors to express ideas without representing real objects. Sculpture is three-dimensional. Architecture balances function and beauty.
boundary_scope.deferred: Abstract art theory, architectural engineering, museum curation.

core_concept: The Orchestra, Keyboard Instruments, and Composers

boundary_scope.in_scope: An orchestra has four sections: strings, woodwinds, brass, and percussion. Keyboard instruments include piano and organ. Famous composers wrote music still performed today.
boundary_scope.deferred: Music theory notation, counterpoint, harmonic analysis.

core_concept: Greek Mythology and American Folk Heroes

boundary_scope.in_scope: Greek myths feature gods (Zeus, Athena) and heroes (Hercules, Odysseus) who explained natural phenomena. American tall tales (Paul Bunyan, Pecos Bill) exaggerate human qualities as folk heroes.
boundary_scope.deferred: Textual criticism, comparative mythology, anthropological analysis.

core_concept: Investigating Forces: Motion, Friction, and Magnetism

boundary_scope.in_scope: Forces cause and change motion. Friction slows moving objects. Magnetism exerts force at a distance without contact. Motion can be predicted from forces.
boundary_scope.deferred: Newton's laws formally, force vectors, torque, magnetic field equations.

boundary_scope.in_scope: Organisms have life cycles (birth, growth, reproduction, death). Organisms have heritable traits. The environment can affect traits. Some traits give survival advantages.
boundary_scope.deferred: DNA and genetics, evolutionary theory, natural selection mechanism.

assumed_experience: knowing animal babies resemble their parents

core_concept: Habitats and Change: Ecosystems Over Time

boundary_scope.in_scope: Living things interact with their environments. Ecosystems change due to natural events or human activity. Fossils and rock layers show how organisms and environments have changed over time.
boundary_scope.deferred: Ecological succession models, species extinction mechanics, paleontology depth.

boundary_scope.in_scope: Earth's atmosphere drives weather through air movement, the water cycle, and solar energy. Climate is the long-term weather pattern of a region. Hazardous weather can be predicted and prepared for.
boundary_scope.deferred: Pressure systems, Coriolis effect, climate modelling equations.

boundary_scope.in_scope: The muscular and skeletal systems move the body together. Eyes and ears are complex sensory organs. The nervous system sends signals to coordinate muscles, senses, and responses.
boundary_scope.deferred: Neuron biology, nerve impulse transmission, vision correction surgery.

core_concept: World Geography: Canada and Important Rivers

boundary_scope.in_scope: Canada is the large country north of the United States. Major rivers (Amazon, Nile, Mississippi, Ganges, Yangtze) shaped the civilizations built along them. Rivers affect geography, climate, and settlement.
boundary_scope.deferred: Drainage basins, river navigation history, geopolitical borders.

core_concept: Ancient Roman Civilization and Byzantine Empire

boundary_scope.in_scope: Rome grew from a city-state to an empire. Romans developed law, engineering (roads, aqueducts), and republican government. The Western Empire fell in 476 CE; the Eastern (Byzantine) Empire lasted until 1453 CE.
boundary_scope.deferred: Latin language, detailed legal codes, military campaigns.

assumed_experience: seeing pictures of the Colosseum or aqueducts
knowing about Roman myths from G2

boundary_scope.in_scope: The Vikings were seafaring people from Scandinavia who explored and traded across Europe, reaching Iceland, Greenland, and North America around 1000 CE.
boundary_scope.deferred: Old Norse language, Viking political systems, archaeological detail.

assumed_experience: hearing about Viking ships and exploration in stories

core_concept: The Earliest Americans: Migration and Indigenous Cultures

boundary_scope.in_scope: Early humans crossed from Asia to North America via a land bridge. Different Native American groups developed distinct cultures across different regions. Spanish explorers also reached the American Southwest.
boundary_scope.deferred: Genetic evidence of migration, detailed tribal histories, colonial law.

assumed_experience: knowing different Native American groups from earlier grades

boundary_scope.in_scope: Spanish explorers settled the American Southwest. Other explorers sought the Northwest Passage through North America to Asia. European exploration led to permanent settlements in North America.
boundary_scope.deferred: Detailed explorer biographies, cartography history, geopolitical competition.

assumed_experience: knowing Columbus sailed west to find Asia
hearing about expeditions seeking new routes

core_concept: The Thirteen Colonies: Life Before the Revolution

boundary_scope.in_scope: Thirteen British colonies formed along the east coast of North America. Southern, New England, and Middle Atlantic colonies had different economies and societies. Colonial life varied by region.
boundary_scope.deferred: Detailed colonial governance, trade laws, religious sectarianism.

assumed_experience: knowing the US was once British colonies

core_concept: Art Elements, Native American Art, Rome and Byzantine Art, Architecture

boundary_scope.in_scope: Art uses light and space to create depth. Native American art reflects cultural traditions and materials. Roman and Byzantine art expressed power and religion through mosaics, sculpture, and monumental buildings.
boundary_scope.deferred: Formal iconography, conservation techniques, art market history.

assumed_experience: seeing pictures of Roman sculptures or mosaics

core_concept: Elements of Music, Orchestra, Composers, Musical Connections

boundary_scope.in_scope: Music has melody, harmony, rhythm, and timbre. The orchestra has string, wind, brass, and percussion sections. Composers shape music history. Music connects across cultures and time periods.
boundary_scope.deferred: Music theory notation, counterpoint, biographical analysis.

assumed_experience: listening to music from different periods

core_concept: Myths, Mythical Characters, and Literary Terms

boundary_scope.in_scope: Myths from many cultures (Greek, Roman, Norse) explain natural phenomena and teach values through gods and heroes. Literary terms: theme, conflict, narrator, point of view, symbolism.
boundary_scope.deferred: Comparative mythology scholarship, literary criticism methods.

assumed_experience: reading or hearing myths from different cultures
knowing stories have themes and characters

boundary_scope.in_scope: Energy exists in forms (kinetic, potential, thermal, sound, light). Energy transfers between objects and transforms between forms. Collisions transfer energy. Engineering uses energy transformation to solve problems.
boundary_scope.deferred: Thermodynamics laws, energy conservation equations, power calculations.

assumed_experience: seeing a ball roll after being pushed

core_concept: Investigating Waves: Sound, Light, and Information

boundary_scope.in_scope: Waves transfer energy without transferring matter. Sound waves travel through vibration. Light waves travel through space. People use waves to transfer information (radio, phone, internet).
boundary_scope.deferred: Wave equations, frequency calculations, electromagnetic spectrum depth.

assumed_experience: throwing a stone in water and seeing ripples

core_concept: Structures and Functions of Living Things

boundary_scope.in_scope: Body structures relate to their function. Eyes and ears have specific structures for sensing light and sound. Organisms respond to stimuli through nervous-system pathways connecting sense organs to muscles.
boundary_scope.deferred: Cell biology, molecular structures, biomedical engineering.

assumed_experience: knowing eyes have a lens that focuses light

boundary_scope.in_scope: Earth has a layered structure (core, mantle, crust). Geological processes (erosion, uplift, volcanism) have changed Earth's surface over billions of years. Geologic events (earthquakes, volcanoes) affect people and communities.
boundary_scope.deferred: Plate tectonics mechanics, radiometric dating, seismology equations.

assumed_experience: seeing news about earthquakes or volcanoes

boundary_scope.in_scope: Natural resources can be renewable (wind, solar, water) or nonrenewable (coal, oil, natural gas). Burning nonrenewable fuels releases pollution. Renewable energy is sustainable.
boundary_scope.deferred: Energy economics, policy analysis, carbon cycle chemistry.

assumed_experience: seeing solar panels or wind turbines

boundary_scope.in_scope: The respiratory system (lungs, airways) brings oxygen in and removes carbon dioxide. The circulatory system (heart, blood vessels) transports oxygen and nutrients to all cells.
boundary_scope.deferred: Biochemistry of gas exchange, cardiac physiology, medical procedures.

assumed_experience: noticing breath rate increase during exercise

core_concept: World Geography: Mountains and Mountain Ranges

boundary_scope.in_scope: Mountains form from tectonic plate collisions and volcanic activity. Major ranges (Himalayas, Alps, Rockies, Andes) affect climate, culture, and settlement. The world's highest peak is Everest in the Himalayas.
boundary_scope.deferred: Plate tectonics mechanics, altitude effects on biology, mountaineering.

assumed_experience: knowing the Himalayas are the tallest mountains

boundary_scope.in_scope: After Rome's fall, medieval Europe developed feudalism (lords and serfs), the Christian Church's influence, the Norman Conquest, and growing towns. England developed early law and parliament (Magna Carta).
boundary_scope.deferred: Detailed feudal economics, Church doctrine, parliamentary law depth.

assumed_experience: knowing about the Middle Ages from stories

core_concept: The Spread of Islam and the Holy Wars

boundary_scope.in_scope: Islam, founded by Muhammad in Arabia in the 7th century, spread rapidly across the Middle East, North Africa, and into Europe. Islamic civilization advanced science, math, and philosophy. The Crusades were religious wars over the Holy Land.
boundary_scope.deferred: Quranic scholarship, detailed Crusade campaigns, political theology.

assumed_experience: knowing there are many world religions

boundary_scope.in_scope: Africa has diverse geography (Sahara, savanna, rainforest, coast). Early kingdoms (Kush, Aksum) and medieval kingdoms (Ghana, Mali, Songhai) built wealth through gold and salt trade.
boundary_scope.deferred: Detailed archaeological evidence, language families, colonial impact.

assumed_experience: knowing Africa is a large continent

boundary_scope.in_scope: China was ruled by dynasties over thousands of years. The Mongols (Genghis Khan, Kublai Khan) conquered China. Chinese inventions (printing, gunpowder, compass) changed the world.
boundary_scope.deferred: Dynastic succession politics, Silk Road economics, Chinese language.

assumed_experience: knowing about the Great Wall from G2

boundary_scope.in_scope: Tensions over taxation without representation escalated into the Revolutionary War (1775-1783). American independence was won. Key figures: Washington, Jefferson, Franklin. The French and Indian War set the stage.
boundary_scope.deferred: Loyalist perspective, detailed battles, economic analysis of taxation.

assumed_experience: knowing about the Revolution from earlier grades

core_concept: Constitutional Government and the Declaration of Independence

boundary_scope.in_scope: The Declaration of Independence stated natural rights (life, liberty, pursuit of happiness). The Constitution created three branches of government. The Bill of Rights listed protected freedoms.
boundary_scope.deferred: Constitutional law depth, federalism theory, amendment processes.

assumed_experience: knowing about the Constitution from G2

core_concept: Early Presidents, Reformers, and American Symbols

boundary_scope.in_scope: The first presidents shaped US government and foreign policy. Reformers (abolitionists, suffragists) worked to expand rights. American symbols (flag, eagle, Statue of Liberty) represent national values.
boundary_scope.deferred: Detailed presidential policies, reform movement historiography.

assumed_experience: knowing Washington was the first president
hearing about people who fought for freedom

core_concept: Art of the Middle Ages, Islam, Africa, China, and a New Nation

boundary_scope.in_scope: Medieval European art served religious purposes (illuminated manuscripts, stained glass). Islamic art uses geometric patterns and calligraphy. African and Chinese art reflect cultural values and materials. Early American art documented the new nation.
boundary_scope.deferred: Iconography, art market history, conservation techniques.

assumed_experience: seeing stained glass in a church

core_concept: Elements of Music, Vocal Ranges, Orchestra, and Composers

boundary_scope.in_scope: Voice types range from soprano to bass. The orchestra has string, wind, brass, and percussion sections. Composers from many eras shaped music history through their works.
boundary_scope.deferred: Music theory depth, voice pedagogy, biographical analysis.

assumed_experience: noticing high and low voices in music
hearing different instrument sections in orchestral music

core_concept: Poetry, Fiction, Memoir, Speeches, and Sayings

boundary_scope.in_scope: Poetry uses devices (metaphor, simile, alliteration). Fiction tells invented stories. Memoir is nonfiction about one's own life. Speeches persuade or inspire through rhetorical devices. Sayings and phrases enrich language.
boundary_scope.deferred: Formal rhetoric analysis, publication industry, literary criticism movements.

core_concept: Investigating Matter: Properties, Structure, and Chemical Change

boundary_scope.in_scope: Matter is made of atoms and molecules. Physical changes alter form but not composition. Chemical changes create new substances. Chemistry uses symbols and formulas to represent matter.
boundary_scope.deferred: Quantum mechanics, periodic table depth, molecular orbital theory.

assumed_experience: burning paper and seeing it turn to ash

boundary_scope.in_scope: Plants make energy through photosynthesis. Food chains show how energy flows from producers to consumers. Matter cycles through ecosystems (carbon, nitrogen, water cycles).
boundary_scope.deferred: Thermodynamic efficiency of food chains, biogeochemical cycle equations.

assumed_experience: knowing plants make food from sunlight
knowing animals eat plants or other animals

boundary_scope.in_scope: Earth consists of four interacting spheres: atmosphere (air), hydrosphere (water), geosphere (land/rock), and biosphere (living things). Changes in one sphere affect others.
boundary_scope.deferred: System modelling equations, climate feedback loops, earth science research methods.

assumed_experience: knowing air, water, land, and life interact
seeing how pollution affects rivers and animals

boundary_scope.in_scope: Earth's resources (water, air, land, ecosystems) can be protected or damaged by human activity. Conservation and sustainable practices protect biodiversity and ecosystem health.
boundary_scope.deferred: Environmental law, carbon markets, biodiversity index calculations.

boundary_scope.in_scope: Earth orbits the Sun; the Moon orbits Earth. Seasons result from Earth's tilted axis. Stars are classified by size and brightness. Gravity holds solar systems and galaxies together.
boundary_scope.deferred: Stellar evolution equations, cosmology, telescope optics, space travel engineering.

assumed_experience: noticing day length changes with seasons
seeing planets in the night sky

boundary_scope.in_scope: The endocrine system produces hormones that regulate growth, metabolism, and reproduction. The reproductive system enables continuation of life. Puberty is driven by hormonal changes.
boundary_scope.deferred: Biochemistry of hormone synthesis, fertility medicine, detailed anatomy.

assumed_experience: knowing that people grow and change during adolescence

boundary_scope.in_scope: Great lakes form from glaciation or tectonic processes. The Great Lakes of North America (Superior, Michigan, Huron, Erie, Ontario) are the world's largest freshwater system. Lake Baikal holds the world's largest freshwater volume.
boundary_scope.deferred: Limnology, international water law, climate effects on lake levels.

assumed_experience: knowing the Great Lakes are between the US and Canada

boundary_scope.in_scope: The Maya (Yucatan) built pyramids and developed a complex calendar and astronomy. The Aztec (Tenochtitlan) had a sophisticated city and religious system. The Inca built a road network in the Andes. All three were conquered by Spain in the 1500s.
boundary_scope.deferred: Maya codex decipherment, Aztec cosmology detail, Inca quipu mathematics.

assumed_experience: knowing the Inca lived in the Andes

core_concept: European Exploration, Trade, Colonization, and Slavery

boundary_scope.in_scope: European exploration (1400s-1600s) opened global trade routes. The Atlantic slave trade forcibly moved millions of Africans to the Americas. Colonization created economic exchanges alongside exploitation.
boundary_scope.deferred: Economic analysis, detailed legal frameworks, reparations debates.

assumed_experience: knowing about Columbus and Spanish explorers

boundary_scope.in_scope: The Renaissance revived art, science, and classical learning. Leonardo, Michelangelo, and Galileo exemplify the era. The Reformation challenged the Catholic Church; Luther and Calvin led Protestant movements.
boundary_scope.deferred: Theological debates, art market economics, detailed Reformation politics.

assumed_experience: seeing pictures of Leonardo or Michelangelo's work

core_concept: England from the Golden Age to the Glorious Revolution

boundary_scope.in_scope: Elizabethan England (Queen Elizabeth I) saw flourishing arts and exploration. The English Civil War and Glorious Revolution (1688) established parliamentary power over the monarchy.
boundary_scope.deferred: Parliamentary law details, religious sectarianism, economic history.

assumed_experience: hearing about kings and queens of England

boundary_scope.in_scope: Russia developed from Kievan Rus to the Tsardom. Ivan the Terrible and Peter the Great expanded and modernized Russia. Russia spans both Europe and Asia.
boundary_scope.deferred: Cyrillic language, detailed Orthodox Church history, economic history.

assumed_experience: knowing Russia is a very large country
seeing Russia spanning Asia on a world map

boundary_scope.in_scope: Feudal Japan (1100s-1800s) was ruled by shoguns (military leaders) with samurai warriors serving lords (daimyo). Bushido was the samurai code of honor. Japan was largely isolated from the West for centuries.
boundary_scope.deferred: Edo period economics, Meiji Restoration, samurai arts depth.

assumed_experience: knowing about samurai from movies or stories
knowing Japan is in East Asia

core_concept: Westward Expansion Before and After the Civil War

boundary_scope.in_scope: Manifest Destiny was the belief that the US should expand to the Pacific. Pioneers crossed the continent via trails. After the Civil War, railroads opened the West; Native Americans were displaced onto reservations.
boundary_scope.deferred: Detailed Native displacement law, railroad economics, homesteading policy.

assumed_experience: knowing the US stretches from Atlantic to Pacific
seeing images of wagon trains or railroads

core_concept: The Civil War: Causes, Conflicts, and Reconstruction

boundary_scope.in_scope: Slavery divided the US; the Confederacy seceded. The Civil War (1861-1865) ended with Union victory and abolition of slavery (13th Amendment). Reconstruction tried to rebuild the South and integrate freed Black Americans.
boundary_scope.deferred: Reconstruction politics, Black Codes, economic analysis of Reconstruction.

assumed_experience: knowing about Lincoln and the Civil War from earlier grades

core_concept: Native Americans: Cultures, Conflicts, and US Policies

boundary_scope.in_scope: Native American nations had diverse cultures. US expansion policies (Indian Removal Act, reservation system) displaced nations. Some groups resisted militarily; others negotiated. Native cultures survived despite enormous pressure.
boundary_scope.deferred: Specific treaty law, contemporary Native rights, tribal sovereignty detail.

assumed_experience: knowing about the Trail of Tears

core_concept: Art of the Renaissance, Baroque, and World Traditions

boundary_scope.in_scope: Renaissance art (Leonardo, Michelangelo) used perspective and realism. Baroque art used drama and light (Rembrandt). American 19th-century art documented landscapes. Japanese woodblock prints and Russian art reflect distinct cultural traditions.
boundary_scope.deferred: Formal connoisseurship, auction history, art forgery.

core_concept: Music Eras and American Musical Traditions

boundary_scope.in_scope: Music from different eras (Baroque, Classical, Romantic) reflects its historical context. American musical traditions include jazz, blues, folk, and gospel. Composers build on earlier traditions while innovating.
boundary_scope.deferred: Music theory depth, ethnomusicology, jazz theory.

assumed_experience: knowing music changes over time and place

boundary_scope.in_scope: Drama is writing meant to be performed (plays, scripts). Fiction has subgenres (adventure, mystery, historical). Famous speeches use rhetorical devices to persuade (anaphora, repetition, appeal to values).
boundary_scope.deferred: Theatrical history, dramatic theory, speech criticism depth.

assumed_experience: hearing a famous speech (MLK, Lincoln, etc.)

boundary_scope.in_scope: Light is an electromagnetic wave. The electromagnetic spectrum includes radio, microwave, infrared, visible, ultraviolet, X-ray, and gamma waves. Light interacts with matter through absorption, reflection, and refraction.
boundary_scope.deferred: Maxwell's equations, quantum optics, photon physics.

assumed_experience: knowing sunlight splits into a rainbow
using a camera or phone screen

boundary_scope.in_scope: Thermal energy is the total kinetic energy of particles. Heat transfers by conduction, convection, and radiation. Temperature measures average particle energy. Ocean currents redistribute thermal energy globally.
boundary_scope.deferred: Thermodynamics laws, Carnot efficiency, entropy.

assumed_experience: feeling conduction through a metal spoon in hot soup

boundary_scope.in_scope: The water cycle (evaporation, condensation, precipitation) drives weather. Climate describes long-term patterns. Climate is affected by latitude, altitude, oceans, and human activity.
boundary_scope.deferred: Atmospheric chemistry, climate modelling equations, paleoclimatology.

assumed_experience: knowing some places are always cold or hot

boundary_scope.in_scope: Earth's crust is divided into tectonic plates that move. Plates collide, separate, or slide past each other, causing earthquakes, volcanoes, and mountain formation. Rocks cycle through igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic forms.
boundary_scope.deferred: Plate motion velocities, radiometric dating, seismograph reading.

assumed_experience: seeing different rock types on hikes

boundary_scope.in_scope: Natural hazards include landslides, floods, hazardous weather (hurricanes, tornadoes), and earthquakes/volcanoes. Human societies can prepare for but not prevent natural hazards.
boundary_scope.deferred: Hazard probability calculations, insurance economics, disaster policy.

assumed_experience: hearing about hurricanes or earthquakes in the news

boundary_scope.in_scope: Cells are the basic unit of life. Cells divide (mitosis) to grow and repair tissue. Cells organize into tissues, organs, and organ systems. All living things are made of one or more cells.
boundary_scope.deferred: Meiosis, DNA replication, cell signaling pathways, cancer biology.

assumed_experience: knowing the body is made of cells
having used a microscope in science class

boundary_scope.in_scope: Deserts cover about one-third of Earth's land. The Sahara (Africa), Gobi (Asia), Arabian, and Atacama are major deserts. Deserts form from rain shadows, trade winds, and distance from oceans.
boundary_scope.deferred: Desertification processes, desert ecology depth, historical trade routes.

assumed_experience: seeing pictures of the Sahara Desert
knowing deserts are very dry and hot or cold

core_concept: Lasting Ideas from Ancient Greece and Rome

boundary_scope.in_scope: Greek democracy, philosophy (Socrates, Plato, Aristotle), literature (Homer), and science laid foundations for Western civilization. Roman law, engineering, and governance created frameworks still influential today.
boundary_scope.deferred: Ancient language study, detailed philosophical systems, textual criticism.

assumed_experience: knowing about Greek myths and democracy

boundary_scope.in_scope: The Enlightenment (17th-18th century) emphasized reason, individual rights, and scientific thinking. Thinkers (Locke, Rousseau, Voltaire) challenged monarchy and religion. Enlightenment ideas inspired the American and French Revolutions.
boundary_scope.deferred: Detailed philosophical texts, printing press economics, religious counterreforms.

assumed_experience: knowing Enlightenment ideas inspired the US Constitution
knowing science changed how people understand the world

boundary_scope.in_scope: The French Revolution (1789) overthrew the monarchy and established a republic based on liberty, equality, and fraternity. Napoleon Bonaparte rose to power, reformed France, and conquered much of Europe.
boundary_scope.deferred: Terror period politics, Napoleonic law codes, economic causes.

assumed_experience: knowing revolutions happen when people want rights

boundary_scope.in_scope: The Industrial Revolution mechanized production, creating factories, urban growth, and new working conditions. Capitalism is based on private ownership and markets. Socialism responds to capitalist inequality. Marx and Engels critiqued capitalism.
boundary_scope.deferred: Economic theory depth, labor law history, Cold War ideological analysis.

assumed_experience: knowing about rich and poor inequality

boundary_scope.in_scope: Inspired by American and French Revolutions, Latin American colonies gained independence in the early 1800s. Bolívar and San Martín led independence movements in South America. Mexico gained independence from Spain in 1821.
boundary_scope.deferred: Post-independence politics, economic dependency, caudillo history.

assumed_experience: knowing Spanish is spoken in many countries south of the US

boundary_scope.in_scope: From 1870-1920, millions of immigrants came to American cities for factory work. Industrialization created wealth but also poor working conditions. Progressive reformers pushed for labor laws, voting rights, and regulation.
boundary_scope.deferred: Immigration law details, labor union history, trust-busting economics.

assumed_experience: knowing many Americans have immigrant ancestors

core_concept: Art History Survey: Classical to Post-Impressionism

boundary_scope.in_scope: Major Western art movements: Classical (Greece/Rome), Gothic (cathedrals), Rococo, Neoclassical, Romantic, Realist, Impressionist (Monet, Renoir), Post-Impressionist (Van Gogh, Cézanne). Architecture evolved alongside.
boundary_scope.deferred: Market value, forgery, conservation, art criticism methodology.

assumed_experience: recognizing Monet or Van Gogh paintings

boundary_scope.in_scope: Baroque music (Bach, Handel) used counterpoint and ornamentation. Classical period (Mozart, Haydn) valued clarity and balance. Romantic music (Beethoven, Chopin) expressed emotion with complex orchestration.
boundary_scope.deferred: Music theory analysis, score reading, performance practice history.

assumed_experience: hearing Bach or Beethoven in films or concerts
knowing music has different historical eras

core_concept: Fiction, Nonfiction, Drama, Essays, and Speeches

boundary_scope.in_scope: Literary nonfiction (essays, speeches) argues or reflects on real topics. Drama is written to be performed. Fiction includes novels and short stories. Literary analysis examines how authors achieve their effects.
boundary_scope.deferred: Rhetoric theory depth, publishing history, academic literary criticism.

assumed_experience: knowing the difference between fiction and nonfiction

boundary_scope.in_scope: Humans migrated from Asia to the Americas tens of thousands of years ago. Indigenous societies developed across North, Central, and South America with diverse cultures. The Vikings reached North America around 1000 CE.
boundary_scope.deferred: Genetic migration studies, detailed tribal histories, Viking settlement archaeology.

assumed_experience: knowing Native Americans lived in America before Europeans

core_concept: European Exploration and Colonization of the Americas

boundary_scope.in_scope: Columbus (1492) opened the Americas to Europe. Spanish (Cortés, Pizarro), French, and English explorers colonized different regions. The Columbian Exchange traded plants, animals, and diseases between hemispheres.
boundary_scope.deferred: Disease impact quantification, colonial legal frameworks, geopolitical competition.

assumed_experience: knowing about Columbus and Spanish exploration from earlier grades

boundary_scope.in_scope: English settlers established 13 colonies along the east coast. Southern colonies relied on tobacco and slavery. New England colonies were founded by Puritans. Middle colonies had diverse populations and mixed economies.
boundary_scope.deferred: Detailed colonial governance, religious sectarianism, colonial trade economics.

assumed_experience: knowing about Plymouth and the Pilgrims

boundary_scope.in_scope: The French and Indian War left Britain in debt; it taxed the colonies without representation. Colonial protests escalated into the Revolutionary War. Common Sense (Paine) and the Declaration of Independence shaped the break from Britain.
boundary_scope.deferred: Loyalist perspective, detailed battles, economic analysis of taxation.

assumed_experience: knowing about the Revolution from earlier grades
knowing about Thomas Paine and Jefferson

boundary_scope.in_scope: The Articles of Confederation failed; the Constitutional Convention (1787) created a new government. The Constitution established three branches, federalism, and separation of powers. The Bill of Rights (1791) added protections.
boundary_scope.deferred: Federalist Papers depth, anti-Federalist arguments, constitutional law.

assumed_experience: knowing about the Constitution from earlier grades

core_concept: The New Republic and the War of 1812 (1789-1820s)

boundary_scope.in_scope: The first presidents (Washington, Adams, Jefferson, Madison) set precedents for the republic. The War of 1812 with Britain ended in stalemate and reinforced American national identity.
boundary_scope.deferred: Detailed presidential policies, Hamilton-Jefferson debates, Native American diplomacy.

assumed_experience: knowing about Washington and Jefferson from earlier grades
knowing about the War of 1812 from G2

core_concept: Westward Expansion Before the Civil War (1820s-1860)

boundary_scope.in_scope: Manifest Destiny drove American expansion west. The Louisiana Purchase doubled US territory. The Oregon Trail and Gold Rush drew settlers. Texas and California were acquired from Mexico. Native Americans were pushed onto reservations.
boundary_scope.deferred: Mexican-American War detail, treaty law, economic geography.

assumed_experience: knowing the US expanded to the Pacific
knowing about the Oregon Trail from G5

boundary_scope.in_scope: Slavery's expansion into new territories divided the nation. Lincoln's election prompted Southern secession. The Civil War (1861-1865) ended slavery. Reconstruction failed to secure Black civil rights long-term.
boundary_scope.deferred: Black Codes, Reconstruction amendments, political analysis.

assumed_experience: knowing about Lincoln and the Civil War from earlier grades

core_concept: Westward Expansion After the Civil War (1860s-1877)

boundary_scope.in_scope: The Transcontinental Railroad (1869) connected the coasts. Homesteaders settled the Great Plains. The American Indian Wars ended with Native nations confined to reduced reservations and bison herds nearly destroyed.
boundary_scope.deferred: Railroad economics, homestead land policy, detailed battle histories.

boundary_scope.in_scope: Mass immigration transformed American cities (1880s-1910s). Industrialization created a factory economy and working class. Urban growth brought poverty, tenements, and reform pressure. Robber barons built monopolies.
boundary_scope.deferred: Immigration law history, trust-busting detail, labor union economics.

assumed_experience: knowing many Americans have immigrant roots
knowing factories and cities grew after the Civil War

boundary_scope.in_scope: Reformers sought civil rights for Black Americans after Reconstruction. The women's suffrage movement won the vote (19th Amendment, 1920). Progressive reformers regulated industry, improved public health, and protected workers.
boundary_scope.deferred: Detailed amendment history, feminist theory, Progressive legislation analysis.

assumed_experience: knowing women weren't always allowed to vote

boundary_scope.in_scope: WWI began from European alliance conflicts; the US entered in 1917. Trench warfare, poison gas, and new weapons caused mass casualties. The Treaty of Versailles imposed harsh terms on Germany. Russia's 1917 revolution created the Soviet Union.
boundary_scope.deferred: Alliance treaty mechanics, military strategy, Wilson's 14 Points.

assumed_experience: knowing there were two World Wars

core_concept: The Twenties and the Great Depression (1919-1939)

boundary_scope.in_scope: The 1920s saw economic prosperity, cultural change (jazz age), and stock market speculation. The 1929 crash caused the Great Depression. Roosevelt's New Deal used government programs to provide relief.
boundary_scope.deferred: Macroeconomics of depression, New Deal program details, Hoover's response.

assumed_experience: knowing about economic crashes and unemployment

boundary_scope.in_scope: WWII began with Nazi Germany's aggression; Japan attacked Pearl Harbor (1941), pulling the US in. Key events: Holocaust, D-Day, Pacific Theater, atomic bomb (1945). The UN was established in the postwar order.
boundary_scope.deferred: Military strategy detail, Holocaust historiography, nuclear ethics.

assumed_experience: knowing about WWII from news, movies, or family history
knowing about Pearl Harbor and the Holocaust

core_concept: Cold War, Vietnam, and the Age of Civil Rights (1945-1975)

boundary_scope.in_scope: The US and USSR entered a Cold War of ideological competition. Korea (1950) and Vietnam (1965-1975) were proxy wars. The Civil Rights Movement (MLK, Rosa Parks) won legal equality for Black Americans. The US landed on the Moon (1969).
boundary_scope.deferred: Cold War proxy strategy depth, Vietnam policy debates, civil rights legislation detail.

assumed_experience: knowing about MLK and Rosa Parks

boundary_scope.in_scope: Post-Vietnam challenges, the Reagan revolution, the end of the Cold War, 9/11 and the War on Terror, social and technological change, and early 21st-century political polarization shaped modern America.
boundary_scope.deferred: Detailed presidential policy analysis, economic statistics, ongoing political events.

assumed_experience: knowing about the internet and smartphones

core_concept: Chemical Reactions and Matter: Atoms, Bonds, Reactions

boundary_scope.in_scope: Matter is made of atoms (protons, neutrons, electrons). Atoms bond to form molecules. Chemical reactions rearrange atoms into new substances. Bonds are ionic (electron transfer) or covalent (electron sharing).
boundary_scope.deferred: Quantum mechanics, molecular orbital theory, reaction rate equations.

assumed_experience: knowing oxygen is in the air

boundary_scope.in_scope: Chemical reactions release or absorb energy. Exothermic reactions release energy (burning); endothermic reactions absorb energy. Chemical energy stored in fuel and food powers living and mechanical systems.
boundary_scope.deferred: Thermochemistry equations, activation energy, catalysis theory.

boundary_scope.in_scope: Metabolism is the set of chemical reactions sustaining life. Cellular respiration converts glucose and oxygen into energy (ATP), water, and carbon dioxide. The digestive system provides glucose; the respiratory system provides oxygen.
boundary_scope.deferred: ATP synthesis mechanism, enzyme kinetics, Krebs cycle details.

assumed_experience: knowing you breathe oxygen and breathe out CO2
knowing food becomes energy in the body

boundary_scope.in_scope: Photosynthesis converts sunlight, CO2, and water into glucose and oxygen. The carbon, nitrogen, and water cycles move matter through living and non-living systems. Photosynthesis and cellular respiration are complementary.
boundary_scope.deferred: Light reaction biochemistry, nitrogen-fixing bacteria species, isotope tracing.

assumed_experience: knowing plants make their own food from sunlight
knowing about the water and carbon cycles

boundary_scope.in_scope: Ecosystems are communities of interacting species and their environment. Population changes (predator-prey cycles) ripple through ecosystems. Human activity and natural events (fire, drought) cause ecosystem change.
boundary_scope.deferred: Population dynamics equations, island biogeography, species interaction models.

assumed_experience: knowing animals depend on each other and their environment
hearing about forest fires affecting wildlife

boundary_scope.in_scope: Humans use natural resources (fossil fuels, minerals, water, timber) and release pollution. Overuse threatens ecosystems and climate. Environmental protection involves policy, technology, and individual choices.
boundary_scope.deferred: Carbon budget calculations, international climate policy, economic tradeoffs.

core_concept: Modern Art: Fauvism, Cubism, Surrealism, Abstract Expressionism

boundary_scope.in_scope: 20th-century art broke from tradition. Fauvism used bold color (Matisse). Cubism fragmented perspective (Picasso). Surrealism explored the unconscious (Dali). Abstract Expressionism focused on emotion and process (Pollock). Organic Architecture integrated buildings with nature (Wright).
boundary_scope.deferred: Art market history, psychoanalytic theory in art, museum curation.

assumed_experience: knowing modern art looks very different from classical art

core_concept: Classical Music: Romantic and Patriotic; American Musical Traditions

boundary_scope.in_scope: Romantic composers (Brahms, Wagner, Tchaikovsky) used music to express emotion and national identity. American traditions (jazz, blues, gospel, folk) grew from African American and immigrant cultures.
boundary_scope.deferred: Music theory depth, score analysis, ethnomusicology.

assumed_experience: knowing jazz and blues are American music forms

core_concept: Fiction, Nonfiction, Drama, Autobiography, and Foreign Phrases

boundary_scope.in_scope: Novels and novellas explore complex characters and plots. Autobiography tells the author's own life story. Elements of fiction: point of view, theme, symbolism. Foreign phrases (e.g., carte blanche, déjà vu, per capita) are commonly used in English.
boundary_scope.deferred: Literary theory movements, translation studies, linguistic borrowing depth.

assumed_experience: knowing the terms déjà vu or et cetera

core_concept: Ancient Civilizations: Mesopotamia, Egypt, and Early Bronze Age

boundary_scope.in_scope: The first civilizations arose in Mesopotamia (Sumerians, Babylonians) and Egypt along river systems. Writing (cuneiform, hieroglyphics), irrigation agriculture, and city-states were foundational innovations. The Bronze Age enabled new technologies and warfare.
boundary_scope.deferred: Archaeological debates, comparative chronology, dynasty lists.

assumed_experience: knowing about ancient Egypt from prior grades
knowing that writing has been around for thousands of years

core_concept: Ancient Asia: Indus Valley, Vedic India, and Early China

boundary_scope.in_scope: The Indus Valley civilization (Harappa, Mohenjo-Daro) had planned cities and writing not yet deciphered. Vedic culture introduced the caste system and Hinduism. Early China (Shang, Zhou dynasties) produced bronze work, writing, and Confucian philosophy.
boundary_scope.deferred: Aryan migration debates, caste origins scholarship, oracle bones.

assumed_experience: knowing China and India are ancient civilizations
knowing about Hinduism and Confucius from G6

core_concept: Ancient Greece: City-States, Democracy, and Classical Culture

boundary_scope.in_scope: Greek city-states (Athens, Sparta) developed contrasting systems: Athenian democracy vs. Spartan militarism. Persian Wars and Alexander the Great spread Greek culture across Eurasia. Greek philosophy (Socrates, Plato, Aristotle) shaped Western thought.
boundary_scope.deferred: Athenian democracy limits, Alexander's administrative systems, Greek philosophy depth.

assumed_experience: knowing about ancient Greece and the Olympics

core_concept: Ancient Rome: Republic, Empire, and Fall

boundary_scope.in_scope: Rome grew from city-state to Mediterranean empire. The Republic (Senate, consuls) became the Empire (Julius Caesar, Augustus). Christianity spread under Roman rule. The fall of the Western Roman Empire (476 CE) reshaped Europe.
boundary_scope.deferred: Roman law detail, military strategy, late antique historiography.

assumed_experience: knowing about Julius Caesar and the Roman Empire
knowing Christianity originated during Roman times

core_concept: Origins and Spread of World Religions

boundary_scope.in_scope: Hinduism (Vedic traditions, Brahman, dharma), Buddhism (Siddhartha Gautama, Four Noble Truths), Judaism (monotheism, Torah), Christianity (Jesus, New Testament), and Islam (Muhammad, Five Pillars) each arose in distinct historical contexts and spread across the world.
boundary_scope.deferred: Theological doctrine, sectarian history, interfaith comparisons.

assumed_experience: knowing about major religions from G6 world studies
knowing Christianity and Islam are widely practiced today

core_concept: Byzantine Empire and the Golden Age of Islam

boundary_scope.in_scope: The Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire preserved Greek-Roman culture until 1453 CE. The Islamic Golden Age (750-1258 CE) advanced mathematics, medicine, astronomy, and philosophy. Arab traders spread Islam across Africa and Asia.
boundary_scope.deferred: Byzantine-Islamic theological conflicts, Caliphate succession, Arabic scientific texts.

assumed_experience: knowing the Roman Empire continued in the East
knowing Islamic civilization had great scholars

boundary_scope.in_scope: West African empires (Ghana, Mali, Songhai) controlled trans-Saharan gold-salt trade. Mansa Musa's Mali was one of the wealthiest states on earth. Great Zimbabwe and Swahili coastal cities show diverse African civilizational centers.
boundary_scope.deferred: Oral history traditions, archaeological dating debates, detailed kingdom timelines.

assumed_experience: knowing Africa has ancient civilizations beyond Egypt
hearing about Mansa Musa as the richest person in history

core_concept: Medieval Europe: Feudalism, the Church, and the Crusades

boundary_scope.in_scope: Medieval Europe was organized around feudalism (lord, vassal, serf) and the Catholic Church. The Crusades (1095-1291) were religious wars in the Holy Land. The Black Death (1347-1353) killed one-third of Europe and disrupted feudal order.
boundary_scope.deferred: Crusade historiography, regional feudal variation, Church doctrine.

assumed_experience: knowing about the plague from science class

core_concept: The Renaissance: Art, Humanism, and New Learning

boundary_scope.in_scope: The Renaissance (14th-17th c.) began in Italy as a rebirth of classical learning. Humanism centered human reason and potential. Artists (Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo, Raphael) produced enduring masterpieces. The printing press (Gutenberg) spread new ideas rapidly.
boundary_scope.deferred: Art historical analysis, patronage networks, regional variation.

assumed_experience: knowing about Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo
knowing the printing press changed history

boundary_scope.in_scope: Martin Luther challenged Catholic Church corruption (95 Theses, 1517). Protestant denominations (Lutheran, Calvinist, Anglican) broke from Rome. Religious wars (Thirty Years' War) reshaped European politics. The Counter-Reformation modernized the Catholic Church.
boundary_scope.deferred: Theological doctrine comparison, Peace of Westphalia, denominational splits.

assumed_experience: knowing Protestants and Catholics are different branches of Christianity
knowing Martin Luther started the Reformation

core_concept: Absolute Monarchies and the Scientific Revolution

boundary_scope.in_scope: European monarchs (Louis XIV, Peter the Great) claimed absolute authority. The Scientific Revolution (Copernicus, Galileo, Newton) shifted understanding of the natural world from religious authority to observation and reason. These movements together challenged traditional power.
boundary_scope.deferred: Court politics detail, Newton's mathematics, religious-scientific conflict.

assumed_experience: knowing Galileo was persecuted by the Church

boundary_scope.in_scope: Enlightenment thinkers (Locke, Rousseau, Voltaire) argued for natural rights, democracy, and separation of powers. Their ideas directly inspired the American, French, and Latin American Revolutions (1776-1825). Napoleon spread revolutionary ideals across Europe by conquest.
boundary_scope.deferred: Enlightenment philosophy depth, Napoleonic legal legacy, Haitian Revolution.

assumed_experience: knowing about the French Revolution and Napoleon

core_concept: The Industrial Revolution and Global Capitalism

boundary_scope.in_scope: Industrialization began in Britain (steam engine, factories, textile mills) and spread to Europe, North America, and Japan. It created a global economy, urban working class, and new political ideologies (capitalism, socialism, Marxism). Child labor and urban poverty sparked reform movements.
boundary_scope.deferred: Industrial economics theory, Marx's writings, global trade networks.

assumed_experience: knowing factories and machines changed how goods are made
knowing about coal, steam engines, and railroads

boundary_scope.in_scope: European nationalism unified Germany and Italy. European empires (British, French, Belgian) colonized Africa and Asia (Scramble for Africa). Colonialism extracted resources, imposed cultures, and caused famines. Japan and the US became imperial powers as well.
boundary_scope.deferred: Anti-colonial resistance movements, detailed treaty analysis, Meiji Japan.

assumed_experience: knowing European countries had large empires
knowing about the British Empire and India

core_concept: World War I and the Russian Revolution (World Perspective)

boundary_scope.in_scope: European alliance systems, nationalism, and imperial rivalry caused WWI (1914-1918). The war killed 20 million; empires collapsed (Ottoman, Austro-Hungarian, Russian). The Russian Revolution (1917) created the world's first communist state. The Versailles settlement planted seeds for WWII.
boundary_scope.deferred: Eastern Front detail, Ottoman genocide, Versailles economics.

assumed_experience: knowing about WWI from G7 US History
knowing the Russian Revolution overthrew the Tsar

core_concept: Rise of Totalitarianism and the Interwar Period (1919-1939)

boundary_scope.in_scope: Fascism arose in Italy (Mussolini) and Germany (Hitler/Nazi Party). Stalin's Soviet terror collectivized agriculture at the cost of millions of lives. Japan's militarist government invaded China. The Great Depression fueled extremism worldwide.
boundary_scope.deferred: Fascist ideology depth, Gulag history, Japanese military strategy.

assumed_experience: knowing about Hitler and the Nazi Party
knowing Stalin ruled the Soviet Union

core_concept: World War II: Global Conflict and Its Aftermath

boundary_scope.in_scope: WWII (1939-1945) involved every major power. The Holocaust killed 6 million Jews and millions of others. The Pacific War ended with atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki. The post-war order created the UN, divided Germany, began decolonization, and launched the Cold War.
boundary_scope.deferred: Holocaust historiography, colonial liberation timelines, nuclear ethics.

assumed_experience: knowing about the Holocaust and atomic bombs

core_concept: Cold War, Decolonization, and Late 20th-Century World

boundary_scope.in_scope: US-Soviet rivalry dominated 1945-1991. Decolonization liberated Asia and Africa (India 1947, African independence 1950s-60s). Chinese Revolution (Mao, 1949). Proxy wars (Korea, Vietnam, Afghanistan). Fall of the Berlin Wall (1989), Soviet collapse (1991).
boundary_scope.deferred: Non-Aligned Movement, Sino-Soviet split, detailed decolonization.

assumed_experience: knowing about the Cold War from G7
knowing about Indian independence and Gandhi

boundary_scope.in_scope: Globalization accelerated after the Cold War: internet, international trade, climate change, global pandemics. Challenges: income inequality, terrorism, refugee crises, democratic backsliding. Multinational organizations (UN, WTO, IMF) coordinate international responses.
boundary_scope.deferred: Macroeconomic policy, ongoing geopolitical events, AI ethics.

assumed_experience: knowing about the internet and global trade
knowing about climate change and global warming

core_concept: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

boundary_scope.in_scope: Atoms have a nucleus (protons + neutrons) surrounded by electrons. Elements are pure substances made of one atom type. The periodic table organizes elements by atomic number; periods and groups reveal predictable properties. Isotopes differ in neutron count.
boundary_scope.deferred: Quantum electron orbitals, radioactive decay equations, spectroscopy.

assumed_experience: knowing atoms are the building blocks of matter
knowing protons and electrons from G7

boundary_scope.in_scope: Waves transfer energy without transferring matter. The electromagnetic spectrum includes radio, microwave, infrared, visible light, UV, X-ray, and gamma rays. Electric circuits transfer energy; voltage, current, and resistance obey Ohm's Law. Magnetism and electricity are linked.
boundary_scope.deferred: Maxwell's equations, circuit design, quantum nature of light.

assumed_experience: knowing light is a form of wave
knowing about electrical circuits and batteries

boundary_scope.in_scope: DNA carries genetic information in a double helix of base pairs (ACGT). Genes are segments of DNA that code for proteins. Heredity follows Mendelian patterns (dominant/recessive). Mutations can be beneficial, neutral, or harmful.
boundary_scope.deferred: Protein synthesis mechanisms, epigenetics, CRISPR technology.

assumed_experience: knowing traits are passed from parents to children
knowing DNA is the molecule of heredity

boundary_scope.in_scope: Darwin proposed that species change over time through natural selection: individuals with advantageous traits survive and reproduce more. Genetic variation plus selection drives adaptation. Fossil evidence, comparative anatomy, and DNA all support evolution.
boundary_scope.deferred: Speciation models, evolutionary rates, modern evolutionary synthesis.

assumed_experience: knowing animals have adaptations suited to their environment

boundary_scope.in_scope: 20th-century global art movements built on and reacted to Western modernism. Non-Western traditions (Japanese woodblock, Chinese ink painting, African sculpture) influenced and were transformed by global exchange. Contemporary art uses multimedia, installation, and digital formats.
boundary_scope.deferred: Art market economics, postcolonial art theory, museum history.

assumed_experience: knowing about Picasso and modern art from G7
knowing art comes from many cultures

boundary_scope.in_scope: 20th-century music diversified globally: American jazz and blues influenced world music. Rock and pop spread via radio and later the internet. Classical modernism (Stravinsky, Bartók) broke from tradition. World music traditions (Indian raga, Afrobeat, Latin rhythms) gained international audiences.
boundary_scope.deferred: Music theory systems across traditions, ethnomusicology depth, recording industry history.

assumed_experience: knowing about jazz and blues from G7
knowing about rock and pop music from lived experience

core_concept: World Literature, Rhetoric, and Persuasive Writing

boundary_scope.in_scope: World literature includes great works from multiple traditions (Homer, Dante, Shakespeare, Dostoevsky, Achebe). Rhetoric uses logos (logic), ethos (credibility), and pathos (emotion) to persuade. Analysis of argumentative texts requires identifying claims, evidence, and reasoning.
boundary_scope.deferred: Literary theory movements, translation studies, extended essay form.

assumed_experience: knowing arguments can use different types of evidence
